data.title: Huntington Bancshares: The Street's Wall Of Worry Is The Big Obstacle Now
data.description:
Operating results ultimately drive long-term returns, but sentiment can be a powerful driver in the near term, and that’s a challenge for Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) today. A series of negative guidance updates has shaken investor confidence, and there appear to be growing concerns about Huntington’s near-term organic growth prospects in increasingly competitive Southeastern banking markets. Moreover, it’s pretty common for institutional investors to steer clear of large-scale M&A stories until the integration process is largely complete.

It’s been a while since I’ve updated my thoughts on Huntington’s shares, but the stock has not done especially well in the interim. Up about 25%, HBAN shares have underperformed large bank peers, including rivals leveraged to the Southeast U.S., including Fifth Third (FITB), PNC (PNC), and Truist (TFC).

Increasing competition for both loans and deposits is a credible near-term threat, and integrating large acquisitions always carries risk. On top of that, I would not be surprised to see management take another significant swing at M&A to jumpstart its growth ambitions in Southeastern markets like North Carolina. All of that said, I think HBAN screens as one of the cheaper banks, provided that mid-single-digit core earnings growth is a credible target for the long term.

Respectable Results To Start The Year

The close of the Cadence acquisition in the first quarter renders year-over-year and sequential comparisons largely moot, but Huntington did at least manage to do a little better than expected in terms of core earnings drivers.

Revenue was about 1% better than expected, or a little less than $0.01/share relative to sell-side expectations. Net interest income (FTE basis) was about a half-penny shy of expectations, with a weaker net interest margin (3.24% vs. 3.15% in Q4’25 and sell-side expectations of 4.28%) offsetting slightly better than expected earning asset growth.

Fee-based revenue was a positive driver this quarter (and a key part of the longer-term bull thesis), driving about $0.015/share of upside. Payments and capital markets were both better than expected (by about 7% and 18%, respectively).

A higher opex guide was one of the issues coming out of the fourth quarter earnings report, but Huntington came in better than expected, with a $0.01/sh beat relative to expectations and a 58.3% adjusted efficiency ratio versus a 59.6% expectation.

Pre-provision profits were almost $0.02/share better than expected (or about 4% above sell-side estimates), and that’s a solidly positive result. The bank gave some of that back through higher provisioning (almost $0.01/share), but a lower tax rate still ultimately allowed for a nearly $0.03/share beat relative to expectations.

Guidance Once Again Creates Some Concern

Huntington’s guidance updates in 2026 have so far been one of the significant drivers of the stock’s underperformance, as they’ve mostly all been negative revisions, and the Street seems to be getting concerned as to whether there will be more bad news in the coming months.

Higher opex guidance was an issue coming out of the fourth quarter, with the company then making an intra-quarter update that included lower purchase accounting accretion expectations.

This time around, management lowered guidance to the low end of the net interest income growth range. Management mentioned some “credit philosophy misalignment” with its recently acquired banks (Cadence and Vertex) impacting loan growth but also highlighted increasing competition for loans and deposits across its markets (but likely most intense in Texas and Southeastern markets), with around 5 to 15 bp of pressure on loan spreads and higher deposit costs relative to prior expectations.

At around 85%, Huntington’s loan/deposit ratio isn’t as good as I’d like. I generally don’t get too concerned until that ratio hits 90%, but the reality is that funding limitations are emerging as a challenge for banks in Huntington’s key growth markets, and HBAN’s deposit costs were already a modest concern.

There were some positive offsets in the guidance. Fee income growth is tracking higher than expected, including the key payments and capital markets lines, and management has the flexibility to pull back on opex growth to offset the pressure on net interest income.

Growth, Offset By Elevated Risk

Considering the recent acquisitions of Vertex and Cadence and Huntington’s own organic growth drivers, there are credible reasons to expect this bank to outgrow many of its peers in the coming years. As is usually the case, though, that growth opportunity carries with it some meaningful operational and execution risks.

NIM expansion on repricing of the back book is a credible positive driver, but increased competition for loans (lower spreads) and deposits (higher funding costs) is a threat. Likewise, the bank’s focus on simultaneously building out new lending verticals and expanding its geographic reach can drive above-peer loan growth, but optimizing the balance sheet will likely work against that to some extent.

M&A synergies are another significant potential positive driver, as the company is looking for meaningful core expense synergies (25% plus) in the coming years. Although this isn’t an excessively ambitious goal, relatively large recent deals like Truist’s BB&T/SunTrust merger underscore that it’s easier to set a synergy target than reach it. Likewise, while I don’t discount potential revenue synergies from the deals (extending capital markets, payment, and wealth management services to new customers), there is fierce competition across the region in all of these markets, and retaining customers post-deal can be harder than management teams initially expect.

Another combined growth driver and risk to consider is the possibility of further meaningful M&A. Management hasn’t exactly stamped out the notion of doing more M&A, and I can certainly see how another deal or two would further accelerate the company’s organic growth plans across the Southeast U.S. (markets like North Carolina, Tennessee, Georgia, and Florida).

Given an accommodating regulatory environment today, I can see why Huntington might be inclined to move faster than they otherwise would, but the Street is often skeptical, if not outright bearish, on whole-bank M&A, and another deal close on the heels of the Cadence transaction likely won’t be a positive for sentiment.

The Outlook

I like Huntington’s overall positioning now, particularly as Cadence has skewed the bank a bit more towards commercial lending (where I see more opportunities for better risk-adjusted spreads and service-driven differentiation and growth). I also like how the bank has been prioritizing a controlled buildout of its lending capabilities (adding verticals, typically by hiring away productive loan officers from rivals) and investing in fee-based revenue generators (payments, treasury services, wealth management, and capital markets).

Execution remains the big unknown. I think Huntington has a good management team in place, but the reality is that growth through added middle-market verticals and geographical expansion into Southeastern/Texas markets is a plan that a large number of banks are following. The same is largely true of the growth initiatives in payments, treasury services, wealth management, and capital markets, as it frankly now seems unusual to find a bank in the $100-$300 billion asset tier that isn’t looking to do those things.

Why do I think Huntington can stand apart from the rivals? Strong customer satisfaction marks, like Net Promoter Score (as reported by J.D. Power) and Customer Satisfaction Scores (also reported by J.D. Power), are one important driver, as above-peer scores have in the past correlated with better growth. I also believe HBAN management has focused more narrowly than the Street may be crediting; management here has historically taken an approach of only committing to areas where they can be leaders, and I don’t believe HBAN is trying to be all things to all potential banking customers.

There are certainly some meaningful near-term risks, and HBAN management has already backed off its prior $2.00/share 2027 EPS target. Further negative revisions will not be well-received, and I do see some risk here given loan and funding competition.

That said, I’m expecting organic core earnings growth of around 6% over the next five years and around 4% thereafter. That’s sufficient to support a fair value above $19.50, as well as healthy growth in capital returns to shareholders. I get similar results from a ROTCE-driven P/TBV model (a $19.60 fair value on 17% medium-term ROTCE) and a P/E approach ($19.30 on 12x my 2026 EPS estimate).

The Bottom Line

Along with Pinnacle Financial (PNFP), Huntington stands out as one of the cheapest banks in its comp group relative to standardized approaches like P/E. While I think there are some valid reasons for conservatism (M&A integration/synergy risk, increasingly competitive markets, macro/yield curve unknowns), I also think the current discount is excessive. I don’t necessarily think that Huntington is the best bank stock to own for six-month outperformance, but for investors looking for a name to hold for a few years, I think this one merits consideration and due diligence.

data.title: Trump's Psychedelics Stance Reignites Sector - How Investors Can Benefit
data.description:
Investment Overview - White House / FDA Take Steps To Fast Track Approval Of Psychedelic Depression Meds

Last Monday, in a note for Seeking Alpha, I covered the company GH Research (GHRS), based in Dublin Ireland, and focused on the development of two drug candidates derived from mebufotenin (5-MeO-DMT), a rapid-acting psychedelic drug and serotonin agonist.

I noted the >50% rise in the company's share price across the past month, and explained that these gains were driven by the news that U.S. President Trump would be signing an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to substances used abroad to treat post-traumatic stress disorder.

A White House statement noted that over 14m American adults have a serious mental illness, with 8m currently taking prescription medicine, that suicide rates had increased by 37% between 2000 - 2018, that veterans "suffer in greater measure from this tragedy," and that:

Psychedelic drugs, including ibogaine compounds, show potential in clinical studies to address serious mental illnesses for patients whose conditions persist after completing standard therapy.

Indeed, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to specific psychedelic drugs, and there are numerous products currently in the clinical trial pipeline for review of safety and efficacy.

It is the policy of my Administration to accelerate innovative research models and appropriate drug approvals to increase access to psychedelic drugs that could save lives and reverse the crisis of serious mental illness in America.

The Executive Order makes several pledges, perhaps most crucially, that:

The Commissioner of Food and Drugs shall provide Commissioner’s National Priority Vouchers to appropriate psychedelic drugs that have received a Breakthrough Therapy designation and are in accordance with the criteria of the National Priority Voucher Program.

This could potentially see psychedelic drugs approved in 1-2 months, as opposed to the standard review period of ~10 months after receipt of a New Drug Application ("NDA").

Furthermore, the Order states that:

The Attorney General shall, in consultation with HHS, initiate and complete review of any product containing a Schedule I substance that has successfully completed Phase 3 clinical trials for a serious mental health disorder, so that rescheduling, if appropriate under 21 U.S.C. 811, may proceed as quickly as practicable for such specific products that are ultimately approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

Again, this is crucial because psychedelic drugs are classified as schedule I substances, which means that they are considered to have no medical use, and may not be prescribed, marketed or sold in the United States.

Without rescheduling, a psychedelic drug product would stand almost no chance of securing commercial approval, but now it seems as though the government is opening the door for multiple companies developing such products to push to have their drugs approved.

Besides GH Research, there are several listed companies investors should be aware of whose chance of becoming commercially successful have drastically improved following last week's news. For example, Compass Pathways (CMPS), AtaiBeckley (ATAI), Definium Therapeutics (DFTX), and Cybin (HELP).

In a statement last week, the FDA commented that:

The FDA is issuing national priority vouchers to three companies studying:

  • Psilocybin for treatment-resistant depression.
  • Psilocybin for major depressive disorder.
  • Methylone for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).

In addition, the agency is allowing an early phase clinical study of noribogaine hydrochloride to move forward following an Investigational New Drug (IND) submission.

In the rest of this post, I will identify and discuss the companies in question, and speculate about which may present the optimal investment opportunity for an investor seeking to gain access to a sector of the stock market that appears to have been handed a new lease of life.

FDA Hands Priority Review Vouchers To Three Biotechs, Including Compass Therapeutics

Last Friday, it was reported that the FDA would hand out Commissioner's National Priority Vouchers ("CNPVs") to three biotechs - Compass Pathways (CMPS), the non-profit Usona Institute, and Transcend Therapeutics - a private biotech that was acquired by Otsuka Pharmaceutical in March.

The obvious choice here would therefore be Compass - its ADR stock is now up >35% year-to-date, and >140% on a one-year basis (at the time of writing), trading at $9.6 pre-market today. Compass' market cap valuation, however, is $920m, and on a five-year basis, it's stock is in fact down >70%.

On Friday, Compass issued a press release, confirming receipt of a CNPV - it's CEO was quoted as follows:

As the most advanced company in the classic psychedelics field, Compass has generated positive data from two large, well controlled Phase 3 clinical trials. Based on the strength of our data, the FDA granted us a rolling NDA submission and review.

Importantly, while the CNPV may provide process efficiencies and accelerated review timelines, an NDA submission must still meet FDA’s established standards of clinical evidence, scientific rigor, and regulatory compliance. We are confident we meet these standards.

Compass's product candidate COMP360 is a synthetic form of psilocybin, a naturally occurring psychedelic compound produced by over 200 species of fungi. When I covered the company for Seeking Alpha in February, it had just announced positive Phase 3 study results for COMP260 in patients with treatment resistant depression ("TRD").

The primary endpoint was the difference in change from baseline in the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) scores between the 25 mg and 1 mg groups at Week 6, and the company noted that:

Two fixed doses – administered 3 weeks apart – of COMP360 25 mg versus 1 mg demonstrated a highly statistically significant reduction in symptom severity with a p-value of <0.001 and a clinically meaningful difference of -3.8 points in change at the primary endpoint.

While this may be sufficient to secure an approval, doubts around the efficacy of COMP360 have led to market sell-offs in the past, and analysts have noted (as discussed in my last post) that Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) Spravato, a form of esketamine, approved to treat TRD in 2019 (and as a monotherapy last year), may have a slight edge in this regard.

Besides non-drug related therapeutic options such as cognitive behavioural therapy ("CBT"), and stimulation therapies such as vagus nerve stimulation, and electroconvulsive therapy, Spravato is one of the few options available to treat TRD, and earned revenues of ~$1.7bn in 2025.

The drug can be administered by a licensed healthcare practitioner, with patients monitored for 2-hours post-administration, while patients undergoing therapy with COMP-360 must be monitored for 6 hours, and meet with a therapist prior to treatment.

As we can see above, Compass believes its product may serve a 4m patient market, however there are some restrictions on its growth worth pointing out, for example, a lack of education about the benefits of treatment with psychedelics amongst patients and physicians, a protracted treatment and monitoring process, and a paucity of treatment centers available at this time.

Nevertheless, Compass management must now feel that a potential approval is tantalizingly close, and if its therapy is reclassified from Schedule I to Schedule II, III, or IV, it would be far more simple to transport, distribute, and administer its therapy, and with government backing, to market it to physicians and patients also.

I'd also speculate that insurers would be open to providing reimbursement for COMP360, if approved, and although I am not aware of any discussion around the price of the therapy, Spravato apparently costs ~$4k - $6k uninsured in the first month (when ~8 doses are administered), the $1k - $1.8k, or ~$500 - $1k in the first month, and $200 - $600 thereafter.

Given COMP-360 lasts "at least 26 weeks after 1 or 2 doses (source: compass corporate presentation), I wonder if the therapy could potentially undercut Spravato in terms of annual cost?

It may be a close run thing, but either way, I don't find it hard to plot a course to a "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) market opportunity for psychedelic drug treatment for TRD patients, and potentially, for patients with other forms of depression, such as major depressive disorder, an indication in which AbbVie's (ABBV) med Vraylar earned ~$3.6bn of revenues last year.

As such, my take is that, while approval is by no means guaranteed - Compass itself points out the FDA's review process is likely to be no less rigorous despite being shorter - Compass <$1bn market cap valuation at the time of writing may significantly undervalue the business, and I'd expect plenty of share price volatility, trending to the upside, as the company shares more updates on the progress of any approval application.

Other Competitors That May Challenge Compass, GH Research

While Compass' receipt of a CNPV may put it in pole position to secure a historic first approval for a psychedelic med, there are many other companies that will be hoping to secure their own CNPVs before long.

For example, AtaiBeckley stock is now up >35% on a one-month basis, and up >200% on a one-year basis. I covered the company in October last year, assigning stock a Buy rating, although shares are in fact down ~10% since.

ATAI has two candidates targeting patients with TRD - BPL-003, a mebufotenin nasal spray, and VLS-01, a DMT buccal film. The company plans to initiate a Phase 3 study for BPL-003 this quarter after completing an end-of-phase 2 meeting with the FDA. In its Phase 2a, open label proof of concept study, the company observed that:

Following the first (8 mg) dose, patients experienced a mean MADRS reduction of 13.3 points from baseline at Day 2 and a mean MADRS reduction of 12.9 at Day 8. One week after the second dose (12 mg), there was a further decrease in MADRS score to a total of a 19.0-point reduction from baseline, with sustained antidepressant effects observed through Week 12 (13.7 points from baseline).

Apparently, patients in the study were able to be discharged 2 hours after therapy, while the efficacy data reported makes a case for a best-in-class therapy, although cross-trial comparisons are generally problematic to make, and not always to be trusted.

In its latest earnings statement, Atai says its twin Phase 3 studies will read out topline data in 2029, which potentially places any approval years away, and years after a potential COMP-360 approval. The company reported a cash position of $85m and short-term securities of $135m as of the end of 2025, which it says is sufficient to see it to the Phase 3 readouts.

While time may be against Atai, its data shared to date does seem to imply it has a shot of one-day challenging for standard-of-care ("SoC") within an emerging psychedelics field. Its market cap is presently ~$1.7bn, which may reflect a more diverse pipeline than e.g. Compass, targeting social anxiety disorder ("SAD") with an MDMA based drug, and sharing Phase 2 TRD data for VLS-01 later this year.

Meanwhile, Definium Therapeutics, formerly known as MindMed, has seen its stock increase by >205% since I issued stock with a Hold rating back in January 2025. The company's lead product is DT120 ODT - lysergide tartrate, the salt form of lysergic acid diethylamide ("LSD"), a synthetic serotonergic psychedelic.

In an investor day press release from earlier this month, Definium's CEO stated:

With three pivotal readouts expected over the next six months, we are rapidly establishing comprehensive clinical evidence for DT120 ODT. Together, these trial outcomes will inform our regulatory approach, including an expeditious path to a potential NDA submission. We continue to execute with focus and urgency to deliver transformative treatments for patients and sustained value for shareholders.”

The readouts are in MDD, Generalized Anxiety Disorder ("GAD"), with another readout in post traumatic stress disorder ("PTSD") due 2027. The company reported a cash position of $412m in a recent investor presentation, and full-year 2025 operating expenses of $166.3m, therefore, although additional fundraisings may be conducted if upcoming data readouts are positive, the company has no immediate financial concerns.

As we can see above, Definium's data in GAD appears to compare favorably to drugs such as Eli Lilly's (LLY) Cymbalta, a drug whose sales are now admittedly negligible due to its patents having expired many years ago. Nevertheless, with its novel mechanism of action ("MoA"), I'd expect DT120 to command a much higher price point than older therapies, with patent protections potentially stretching out many years, and in markets such as GAD and MDD, again, a blockbuster revenue opportunity seems to present itself.

I'd speculate that Definium could be pushing for a CNPV in 2026, although with its market cap now standing at $2.24bn, it's unclear how much more upside shares could explore.

Finally, Cybin (HELP), which has recently rebranded to Helus Pharma, issued a statement in response to President Trump's Executive Order last week. The CEO is quoted as follows:

Our lead program, HLP003, currently in Phase 3 development, has received FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for the adjunctive treatment of major depressive disorder, reflecting the urgent need for improved treatments for depression, including the ongoing epidemic of suicide in the United States

Its lead product is a deuterated Novel Serotonergic Agonists ("NSA"), HLP003 which will report data from a Phase 3 study in adjunctive treatment of MDD in Q4, according to a corporate presentation. This therapy is orally administered, although a second candidate, administered intramuscular, targets GAD.

The company's CEO recently stepped down, at the request of the Board of Directors, with executive chairman Eric So resuming his role as interim chief executive officer.

As we can see above, management believes its deuterated candidate offers plenty of competitive advantages over psilocybin, and says HLP003 has achieved a mean 23-point reduction in MADRS at 12 months in a Phase 2 study.

The company reported a cash position of $195m as of the end of 2025, a a net loss of $(42.7m) for Q4 2025. The Phase 3 results due Q4 2026 would appear critical to the company's chances of both raising fresh funding, and securing a CNPV, perhaps early in 2027.

Concluding Thoughts - A Psychedelic To Suit Every Taste?

In summary, then, last week's news has reignited a sector of the stock market that was weighed down by the classification of psychedelics as Schedule I substances, which seemed to make any approval submission from any company unlikely to succeed, and the commercial opportunity significantly restricted.

Now, it seems possible that several therapies could see their approval submissions fast-tracked, and with so many Americans seeking treatment for mental conditions that physicians and pharmaceutical companies struggle to find effective therapies for, the market opportunity in front of the companies covered in this post seems undeniable.

Which company should an investor pick, however? It is difficult to say, and in this post I have tried to be objective and explain each companies position in terms of the progress of their therapies, data shared to date, and upcoming catalysts.

I would finally summarize the opportunities in play as follows. Compass targets TRD, a smaller market than MDD, or even GAD, perhaps, but still large enough to support blockbuster revenues in my view, and it has secured its CNPV. The company has ~$150m funding, which management says will last into 2028, and its stock and valuation are substantially lower than the other companies discussed. Therefore, investors willing to take on some risk and with a short-term investment horizon may find this opportunity suits them best.

ATAI has multiple candidates, strong efficacy data (in my view), and a 2-hour treatment window, but a longer wait for pivotal study data, with sufficient funds to last into 2029, when it could launch an approval submission. It's higher market cap and lengthy studies seem to imply that sudden upside is less likely, but as a longer-term buy and hold, it certainly intrigues.

Likewise, Definium's higher market cap may make sudden share price spikes less likely, although its target markets are larger and more diverse, data strong, and near-term catalysts intriguing - this feels like arguably the least risky longer-term play.

Managerial overhauls and limited funding may count against Cybin / Helus, however a major data catalyst due Q4 could open up a quick path to approval.

Finally, as I wrote about GH Research last week:

GH Research is now confident that it has a "best-in-class" TRD therapeutic treatment, a best-in-class psychedelic, and a best-in-class molecule, and the company believes that, should GH001 be approved, it would qualify for five years' exclusivity in the U.S. (i.e., no generic versions for GH001 could be launched by competitors) and ten years in Europe if approved in that territory, while the product would also be patent protected, including its novel use as an inhalable product, its aerosol composition, and as a medical device.

In summary, there is plenty for prospective investors to mull over, and I would urge them to conduct their own due diligence, as this note provides overviews only, and each candidate provides a different package of efficacy / safety, convenience, durability, and stage of development.

It's also important to note that a new government administration may have the power to row back on the Executive Order, and be more circumspect around changing Schedule's from I to II, III, or IV, and that this could lead to sharp sell-offs in these companies stock prices.

Nevertheless, Psychedelics has become a hot space to watch, where once it seemed obstacles to approval may be too great. I'm excited to see how these various companies plans play out, and at this stage, I'd be watching Compass most closely, while paying close attention to the Phase 3 readouts for Definium and Cybin / Helus.

data.title: War, Fear, And The Data: Why The Seeking Alpha Quant System Says "Don't Sell"
data.description:
Follow Steven Cress on Seeking Alpha

Read Steven Cress' Article on Seeking Alpha

Explore Alpha Picks Today!

Join Pro Quant Portfolio Now!

Sign Up For Premium Today!

Nicole Benjamin: Hey everybody. It's Nicole Benjamin, your host here at Seeking Alpha to bring to you a new series, The Weekly Grade with Steven Cress, where we are going to be giving you key market signals in minutes. Now, some housekeeping before we get started.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

All right. Well, with that out of the way Steven Cress, everyone.

Steven Cress: Good disclaimer.

NB: Thank you. Steven Cress, everyone, VP of Quantitative Strategy here at Seeking Alpha. He is known for some really great products we have on the site, Alpha Picks, PRO Quant Portfolio, and if either of those things sound like something that might be right for you, a more simplified way of investing, go ahead, take a look, see if that's something right.

Alright. Now, to jump right in here, Steve, I have my first question that I want to ask you. With the current war in Iran that's causing so much fear, there's many investors that see this as a time where they need to sell everything and hide in cash. So, with that in mind, how do you use the data to ignore all the scary headlines?

SC: Yeah. I actually try to encourage people to pay attention to the data and ignore the scary headlines. And you're absolutely right. A lot of fear and concern is generated. You have a lot of talking heads on Wall Street, on various TV stations or the newspapers, and they're instilling quite an amount of fear into individuals who actually do act on what they hear and they sell. And typically when they sell, they sell at the bottom. And having done this for a very long time, being in the world of finance for over 35 years and having really much of my career revolve around quantitative analysis and strategies, I'm able to really keep my finger on the pulse of markets during these periods.

And what I have found is that during these periods, during these pullbacks and corrections, you should always continue to add to stocks that have really strong fundamentals because what happens is you will find early during these corrections, people sell-off those stocks and they move into cash, they move into gold, they move into utilities, consumer staples, and they do it because of fear. So, they end up selling really good positions just to make – be in a safe haven. And that's a big mistake. If you can maintain a discipline and continue to look for stocks with good fundamentals and ignore all the white noise around you, that will help build generational wealth over time. And we have many examples in some of our other webinars and articles where we point to that. So, the key is, is to stay disciplined, ignore the talking heads, and follow the data.

NB: Alright. Now, for my next question, you've identified unstoppable stocks like Celestica and Sterling that have crushed the market over the last five years. But what is the one fundamental trait that keeps them resilient? And what do you say to those investors that feel like they've missed a boat because these stocks are trading at just all-time highs?

SC: Yeah. I would say, if you see a Quant Strong Buy recommendation, you have not missed the boat, that is our directional signal that the security is still mispriced. And typically, the common thread that these stocks have, especially over this five year period, is that they consistently have beat both top and bottom line being revenue and earnings per share growth expectations. So, analysts will have estimates on companies for their upcoming quarters, the company's report, and they beat those analyst’s expectations.

And these companies have done it very consistently over a five year period, and they all possess really stellar growth rates. And that's what helps them move ahead whether we're in a bull market or a bear market. Sometimes you'll find in a bull market, they may pull back a lot. Again, we’re talking about fear. People often sell stocks where they made money, but as soon as that fear fades and fundamentals are normalized again, people jump right back into these stocks and they roar right back up.

NB: Now, a big concern for a lot of investors is buying stocks at the top. So, with stocks like Fabrinet, they have great momentum, but they're trading at a premium. So, how does the Quant system decide when a high price is actually a bargain versus it being a Hold when a stock may just be – just too expensive to touch?

SC: Yeah. Fabrinet and the stock that you're showing here, Celestica, they share that. They're both trading in your all-time highs. Our system will tell you when the stock is a Sell. So, the directional Buy, the Strong Buy that you see should, by example, that valuation grade change to a D-, it would automatically default to a Hold. So, even on our system, it gets to the point where it says, okay, the valuation is just too high. So, if you own the shares, just Hold them. We would not recommend continuing to Buy. And in fact, if you see our directional recommendation go to a Sell, a Strong Sell, that would be the time to Sell it.

And, typically, when we see that, the valuation is too high, the growth picture has changed, and perhaps analysts have lowered their estimates on it. So, it's usually a combination of events that could lead it to go from a Strong Buy to Sell. If it goes from a Strong Buy to a Hold, it's usually just on the valuation line, but our system will tell you, it gets refreshed on a daily basis.

NB: So, is there any one trigger that would change it from a Strong Buy to a Hold in the model during just times of global uncertainty?

SC: Yeah. The valuation grade is a telltale. If that goes for – the stock could be a Strong Buy with valuation grade of a D. But if it goes from a valuation grade of a D to D-, that means that it's just triggered enough that we're saying, okay, let's take a step back and just put a Hold on this stock because maybe the P/E's gotten too expensive or the PEG ratio or the price-to-book or price-to-sales. One of those has been triggered, and it's time to take a step back. And what we're looking at in this chart here is this actually shows Celestica and why we like it so much.

And if you look at the growth, and we have a number of growth metrics from revenue growth to EBITDA growth to earnings per share growth to levered free cash flow growth , you could see it's almost a straight A report card. The only area where it's a little bit lower would be free cash flow per share and operating cash flow per share, but on all the other metrics, it is an A. So, those grades, again, they're sector relative. It tells you, it gives you an instant characterization looking at the top line revenue growth. A-, this is way better than the sector. If you look at earnings per share, A, much better than the sector. And we, really, this is very intuitive because we tell you what the growth point is for the stock, and we tell you what the growth rate is for the sector. So, you could see why those grades are A- because for the stock, it's so much stronger versus the sector. Very intuitive.

NB: Alright. Well, we will leave it right there. Thank you so much Steve for joining us today on The Weekly Grade. data.title: RSP: Why I Am Upgrading This Large-Cap ETF
data.description:
One of my concerns with stocks after a three-year run fueled by the performance of the Magnificent 7 or the Elite 8 was the high concentration of the S&P 500 in just a few stocks. At the end of November, I wrote about the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), calling it a unique and compelling ETF but initiating it with a Hold. Since that article, RSP has outpaced the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY):

The idea to Hold RSP instead of SPY was working very well in Q1, when RSP returned 0.6% as SPY returned -4.4%, but Q2 has seen SPY outperform RSP:

Today, though the price is higher, I am upgrading RSP to Buy. In this article, I explain again the ETF and discuss why I am upgrading it.

An Update on the $87.2 Billion ETF

At the end of November, when I first wrote about RSP, it had $75.2 billion in assets. It has increased by 16% to $87.2 billion. The price is up 5.5%, but the majority of the increase is due to inflows that resulted in share creation. Yes, equal-weight, the methodology of this ETF, has gained popularity in 2026 relative to market-cap weighting, such as SPY or the larger Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) use.

$87 billion is big, but it is much smaller than the assets under management for SPY at $725 billion or VOO at $909 billion. RSP is widely followed, with 22,580 Seeking Alpha followers compared to 20,000 in November (+13%), but SPY (597K vs. 572K, up 4%) and VOO (198K vs. 187K then, up 6%) are much more widely followed.

Invesco (IVZ), which launched RSP in 2003, updates its website for the ETF regularly, and the RSP Fact Sheet, last published as of March 31st, helps understand what the ETF is. Quite simply, RSP tracks an index that is based upon the S&P 500 and updates and rebalances quarterly. The ETF has a management fee of 0.2%, which is quite low compared to most ETFs but certainly higher than SPY (0.0945%) or VOO (0.03%).

The 10 largest stocks in RSP make up just 3% of the ETF currently, while the SPY top 10 comprise 38.3% (as of April 23rd). RSP has the exact same stocks, but the weightings are different in the stocks. The sector weightings are different too. At the beginning of the month, RSP's sector allocations were more equally weighted than the S&P 500:

S&P 500 weightings were a lot heavier on Technology:

The S&P 500 has more than twice as much exposure to Technology than the equal-weighted index. Other large differences (greater than 2%) between the weightings include higher exposures of RSP as of the end of Q1 in Industrials (+6.5%), Real Estate (+4.1%), Utilities (+3.8%), Materials (+3.3%), Financials (+2.9%), and Health Care (+2.1%) as well as lower exposures in Communications Services (6.4%). The S&P 500 has eleven GICS, and the weightings in two are substantially lower for RSP and substantially higher for six.

One of the interesting things about sector performance for the S&P 500 in 2026 is that the largest sectors in the S&P 500 have generally underperformed the smaller ones. Here are the returns year-to-date on the 11 State Street ETFs that track these sectors of the S&P 500 (sorted by weight from largest to smallest):

So, four of the five largest sectors in the S&P 500 are down year-to-date as we approach the end of April. The largest sector is up, which is helping the S&P 500 return about 5% year-to-date. Not all the smaller sectors are outpacing Technology, but three are.

RSP has the highest exposure to Industrials, which are up more than twice as much as the S&P 500, but its second largest, Financials, are trailing badly. Its fourth largest, Health Care, is also performing poorly in the S&P 500 by market-cap weighting.

Kudos to Invesco for offering this equal-weight ETF that is large and increasingly popular. The firm has not had as much success with other equal-weighted ETFs. They do offer ETFs based on GICS, with Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Technology ETF (RSPT) standing out with $4.6 billion in assets under management. I did write about this one three months ago, initiating it with a Hold and calling it a better way to invest in Technology. Since then, it has outpaced State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK), gaining 12.6% compared to the 10.2% advance in XLK. The other equal-weight Sector ETFs are all less than $1 billion in assets. Here are the year-to-date returns for them:

Similar to the market-cap-weighted sectors, there are four equal-weighted sectors that are down in 2026, and they are the same. Interestingly, some of the eleven equal-weighted sector ETFs are doing better than the market-cap-weighted ones, and some are doing worse. Here are the two that are doing better by more than 3% and the three that are doing worse by more than 3%:

I like that the equal-weight Technology companies are outpacing the market-cap-weighted ones and that the very best sector, Energy, has better returns with equal weighting. RSP is seeing its performance get hurt by the performance of Industrials, its largest exposure, relative to SPY and VOO.

Invesco shares metrics for RSP, and the ETF appears to be cheaper than SPY on a PE basis and according to the price-to-book value ratio. Invesco views the P/E ratio as 16.7X as of the end of Q1, which is less than the 22.4X for SPY, according to State Street. The price-to-book value ratio is 3.1X as of the end of March, compared to SPY at 5.2X currently.

RSP Has Underperformed the S&P 500

While RSP is doing better than SPY and VOO in 2026, it has lagged badly since the end of 2022:

Again, this move higher in the S&P 500 was driven by the strong performance of the very largest stocks. Here are the returns of the Elite 8 since the end of 2022:

Only one of the stocks has underperformed SPY, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and it has returned 81.7% even after the rough start to 2026. The other seven have all more than doubled.

For investors who believe that winners keep winning, there are many ETFs that focus on the Magnificent 7, the Elite 8, or the very largest companies. For those that are invested in ETFs or funds that target the S&P 500 and are passive, they are very invested in the Elite 8.

Invesco shares a 10-year chart on the Fact Sheet of the return of RSP compared to the S&P 500 as well as the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index as of the end of Q1:

While the last three years have been terrible for equal weight relative to market-cap weighted, RSP did outperform the S&P 500 in 2022 when the market fell and even outpaced it in the rallying market of 2021 marginally.

Why RSP Can Outpace the S&P 500

Predicting how RSP will perform is challenging. It should track the S&P 500 roughly, but the history shows that it can outpace it in rallying markets or in falling markets, and it can certainly underperform it even in falling markets (like 2018).

2026 is seeing two very strong trends: a preference for smaller companies and very different returns by sector for the S&P 500. April has seen a reversion, with the largest companies rallying and the largest part of the S&P 500, Technology, leading the way. In April, the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) has outpaced VOO and SPY, rallying 11.6%, so this trend continues.

I do like smaller companies generally and have written favorably about two ETFs that I include in my ETF model portfolio, ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers (SMDV) and ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats (REGL), both of which I have rated as Buy. In addition to those two ETFs, I include RSP, which is now slightly larger than both of them.

My model portfolio is a balanced portfolio and tries to outpace the S&P 500 for the equity component. While I think that small caps and mid caps can do better than the S&P 500 going forward, I see less risk in an equal-weighted S&P 500, like RSP. When I think about scenarios going forward for RSP, the three that I am evaluating are:

  1. S&P 500 keeps rallying, with the largest stocks leading the way
  2. S&P 500 falls sharply
  3. S&P 500 doesn't change much, while the trends in place for smaller companies remain in place

The bullish scenario includes presumably inflation decline and AI spending continuing to grow rapidly. The bearish scenario includes interest rates continuing to rise and the economy slowing. In the more neutral scenario, the trends established this year towards smaller stocks being in favor likely continues.

In the first scenario, RSP should lag. Assuming the S&P 500 advances in price by 20% in 2026, the total return would be about 21.0%. The S&P 500 would advance in price by 14.6%, and RSP would probably rally by 11%. This would give RSP a price return of 17.1% for the year and a total return of 18.8%. With this lag in performance, it failed to beat the S&P 500 for the year, losing by about 2%. This would be much better relative performance than the past three years!

In the very bearish scenario, I assume that the S&P 500 will fall back to its lows set in April 2025. This would be a price decline of 32.5%. In this scenario, perhaps RSP would do even better than I project, but I assume RSP would fall 29% to about $143.40, which is below its April 2025 low that was slightly above $150.00. For the full year, the S&P 500 would return -28.5% with dividends included, while the RSP total return would be -20.2%. The first win since 2022 would be an advantage of 8.3% in total return. While this is large, it compares to an underperformance of 12.6% in 2023 and 12.2% in 2024 and is only slightly larger than the underperformance of 6.6% in 2025.

In the neutral scenario, I assume that the S&P 500 ends up staying near where it is now over the balance of the year, which would give a total return for the year of 5.7%. In this scenario, I assume that the very largest stocks do what they were doing in Q1 with the rest of the S&P 500 doing somewhat better. If the top 10 names by market cap were to fall in price by 10%, the other names would rally in total by about 6.2%. If this were to happen, RSP would increase in price from here by about 5.9%.

Using the same weights as I did last week in my REGL upgrade (20% S&P 500 bull, 30% bear, 50% neutral), this works out to an expected price return of -0.65% for RSP. The total return would be 0.50%. This is lower than the return on cash currently, but it looks a lot better than the S&P 500, which would return -3.6% before dividends.

It is hard to get excited by an ETF that I expect in these scenarios will have a probability-weighted total return of just 0.5%. Last week, when I upgraded REGL, my total return using the same weights worked out to be 11%, which exceeds cash and my expected return of the S&P 500. As I have said, though, I am bearish on stocks. RSP, even after this move higher in the market, is still beating SPY and VOO in a rallying market, and I think that there is a lot of room for the trends to continue. The scenarios that I shared were conservative. It is possible that RSP could fall a lot less than I had it falling in my bear scenario, and it could also do a lot better in the neutral scenario. So, I am upgrading RSP to Buy.

Conclusion

I liked RSP enough in late November to write an article on it, and I did include it on my ETF watch list at year-end that now totals 85. I wish I had rated it a Buy five months ago rather than today, and I feel a bit awkward upgrading it at this higher price, especially as I feel even more bearish on stocks today than I did five months ago. I like RSP more than SPY.

When I was conveying my negativity towards stocks in late 2025, I was suggesting cash (and TIPS), but many readers questioned what they were supposed to buy if they wanted or needed to own stocks. I understand this, and I developed a Buy rating for SMDV, which has done very well. I upgraded REGL last week too. I continue to view both of these ETFs as likely to perform better than RSP, but, as I said above, my position size is larger in RSP right now.

Buying RSP makes sense compared to owning SPY or VOO. For investors who are willing to look at smaller market caps, I think that there are better ideas, but RSP should perform better in two of the three scenarios that I discussed, and perhaps I was overly conservative in one or both of those and in my bearish scenario.

data.title: Dodge & Cox International Stock Fund Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Market Commentary

International equity markets declined in the first quarter of 2026, as the conflict in Iran disrupted global energy supply and reversed the rally from the first six weeks of the year. The conflict effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz—a critical transportation corridor for roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas supply—and pushed Brent crude prices above $100 per barrel. The surge in energy prices fueled inflation concerns across both developed and emerging economies.

The quarter unfolded in two distinct phases. Through mid-February, global equity markets rose, with international equities extending their lead over U.S. markets. Within technology, advancements in AI raised concerns about its potential to disrupt industries ranging from software and data providers to commercial real estate and financial services. The escalation of the Iran conflict in late February triggered a broad selloff and wide dispersion at the sector level. In the first quarter, the Energy sector surged 28.5% due to supply disruptions, while more economically sensitive areas declined: Consumer Discretionary fell 13.3% and Communication Services declined 9.5%, as inflation weighed on the economic outlook.

International value stocks continued to outperform growth stocks during the quarter. The MSCI ACWI ex USA Value Index outpaced the MSCI ACWI ex USA Growth Index by almost six percentage points, driven primarily by the Value Index's higher exposure to the Energy sector. 2 Despite this outperformance, the MSCI ACWI ex USA Value trades at 11.6 times forward earnings, a significant discount to its growth counterpart at 17.2 times forward earnings. 3 The trajectory of the conflict, its implications for energy supply, and the potential for sustained higher inflation remain key risks for international equity markets.

Portfolio Strategy

During the first quarter, we found several opportunities to invest in high-quality companies that we believe the market has mispriced due to fears of disruption from AI. For example, we added to the Fund's position in Booking Holdings, the largest online travel agency globally, with particularly strong positions in Europe and parts of Asia. 4 While there are concerns in the market that AI platforms, such as ChatGPT, will disintermediate consumer marketplaces like Booking, we believe this underestimates the durability of Booking's market position, supported by the depth and breadth of services they provide to their accommodations suppliers, the stickiness of their traveler customer base, and the operational complexity of the service they provide. Moreover, we think the company has significant opportunities to improve both its products and cost structure with AI.

Elsewhere in the portfolio, we reallocated capital to companies with fee-based business models such as Brookfield, a leading global alternative asset manager focused on real assets, like infrastructure and real estate. We believe Brookfield offers resilient earnings over our investment horizon, supported by strong fundraising momentum, a dedicated AI infrastructure fund that derisks its core franchise, and significant holdings of income-generating assets. Recent weakness among alternative asset managers, driven by concerns of software exposure in private credit, has created an attractive opportunity given Brookfield's real asset focus and minimal exposure to direct lending.

Outlook

Reflecting our value orientation, the Fund trades at 12.4 times forward earnings, a discount to the MSCI ACWI ex USA at 13.7 times. We believe the portfolio is well positioned across a broad range of economic and market environments. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.
